mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar E-Pace P300e engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E-Pace P300e engine (PT153 with hybrid system) typically costs £2,800‑£4,200 supply only. Fitting adds £1,200‑£1,800. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar E-Pace P300e engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The petrol models are better than the diesels, but the PHEVs do have some fairly common issues. A safety recall (R/2023/088) was issued for the coolant pump drive belt, which can fail and cause rapid overheating and potential fire risk. Other concerns include PHEV software and hybrid system failures, HV battery degradation, and timing chain tensioner wear. With regular maintenance and proper battery care, it can be a reliable powertrain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar E-Pace P300e?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most serious issue is the coolant pump drive belt recall – if the belt fails, coolant flow is lost, causing rapid overheating and potential fire risk. Other concerns include PHEV software and hybrid system failures (the car won't go into EV mode or jumps erratically between petrol and EV), HV battery degradation (reduced electric range), and timing chain tensioner wear causing cold‑start rattle.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E-Pace P300e engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £20,000‑£35,000 E-Pace, a £2,800‑£5,000 recon is 10‑25% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. However, if the HV battery has also failed (costing £2,000‑£5,000+), the total repair cost may be substantial. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing. Extended warranty is highly recommended – repairs can be eye‑wateringly expensive.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E-Pace's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E-Pace P300e uses the PT153 (AJ20P3) 1.5‑litre three‑cylinder turbo petrol engine combined with a 108 bhp electric motor. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The P300e badge itself confirms the powertrain – it's the 309 PS plug‑in hybrid offered in the X540 E-Pace from 2020 onwards.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an E-Pace P300e engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – coolant pump belt (recall), PHEV software updates, timing chain wear (if caught early) – are repairable. HV battery degradation may be covered under warranty if the vehicle is still within the warranty period. However, if the engine suffers major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild. The E-Pace is subject to unexpected component failures, so a good warranty is vital.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar E-Pace P300e Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the E-Pace P300e generation. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. As a PHEV, the hybrid system components (electric motor, battery, inverter) add complexity and cost to any replacement.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Pace P300e engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Jaguar specialist). Hybrid system integration adds to the complexity.

cta.label: → Compare E-Pace P300e engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Coolant Pump Drive Belt Failure — Safety Recall (R/2023/088)
problems.symptoms: Engine overheating warning, coolant loss, steam from under bonnet, potential fire risk — may occur without prior warning. A concern has been identified on Ingenium I4 2.0L Petrol PHEV engines where the coolant pump drive belt can become damaged leading to detachment or destruction.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — recall affects vehicles built from 2019 to 2023.
problems.repairCost: £0 (if recalled — dealer repair free) / £500–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (if out of warranty).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see overheating or steam, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: A safety recall (R/2023/088) has been issued for Ingenium I4 2.0L Petrol PHEV engines where the coolant pump drive belt can become damaged leading to detachment. Where coolant pump drive is lost, there will be a loss of coolant flow, causing the engine to rapidly overheat and subsequently rupture an engine‑mounted plastic elbow in the cooling circuit. Coolant can leak onto the turbocharger heatshield which may lead to a fire in the engine compartment. Check your VIN against the recall database immediately. If you see any red warning lamps or signs of smoke, stop the vehicle and move away.

problems.title: PHEV Software & Hybrid System Failures
problems.symptoms: The car won't go into EV mode, the car jumps from petrol to EV and back again erratically, loss of hybrid drive, engine performance issues when battery is depleted, warning messages.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — affects 2023‑2024 model years in particular.
problems.repairCost: £0–£2,500+ [PLACEHOLDER] (software update may be free; HV junction box or battery replacement can be expensive).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hybrid system warnings appear or if the car won't enter EV mode.

problems.recommendation: A software update (recall H555) has been issued for 23/24 model year cars for the EPIC B control in the Inverter — it is classed as safety due to engine performance; if battery becomes depleted, it may not restart or perform poorly. However, some owners have reported that the update itself caused issues, with high voltage fuses blowing and junction boxes needing replacement. If you experience hybrid system issues, have the vehicle diagnosed by a specialist. Extended warranty is highly recommended — repairs can be eye‑wateringly expensive.

problems.title: HV Battery Degradation / 12V Battery Issues
problems.symptoms: Reduced electric‑only range, longer charging times, hybrid system warnings on the dashboard, the car running only on petrol, 12V battery starting issues.
problems.typicalMileage: 20,000‑50,000 miles (age‑related as much as mileage — the battery is subject to degradation over time).
problems.repairCost: £2,000‑£5,000+ [PLACEHOLDER] (HV battery replacement — if available) / £200–£500 (12V battery replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — battery replacement only (if ignored, the vehicle runs as a conventional petrol only).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if HV battery warnings appear or if electric range drops significantly.

problems.recommendation: HV battery failure is a significant concern on the E-Pace P300e. Some owners have reported the high‑voltage battery requiring replacement, with batteries on back order. The 12V battery is also a known issue. Cold weather affects battery performance significantly — preconditioning using mains power can help restore capacity. If your vehicle displays HV battery warnings or starting issues, have it diagnosed promptly. Check whether your vehicle is still under warranty — battery replacement can be expensive.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (PT153)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on cold start, engine misfires or rough idling, poor acceleration and reduced power, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: £2,800–£5,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: While timing chain issues are more commonly associated with Ingenium diesel engines, the petrol variants can also suffer from tensioner wear. A rattling noise from the engine compartment may be caused by a Variable Camshaft Timing (VCT) unit internal seal that is below specification. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Jaguar E-Pace P300e — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Recall repair is free; out‑of‑warranty repair still cheaper than engine replacement
Software update may be free; HV junction box replacement can be expensive but worth fixing
May be covered under warranty; if not, assess cost vs. vehicle value
Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is almost certain
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Jaguar E-Pace P300e? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Jaguar service history with correct hybrid system maintenance
* Recall (R/2023/088 — coolant pump belt) verified as completed — this is safety‑critical
* Software update (H555) verified as completed — addresses inverter/engine performance issues
* HV battery health check completed and documented
* Extended warranty in place — repairs can be eye‑wateringly expensive
avoidIf: * No evidence of recall completion — coolant pump belt failure is a safety issue
* Any sign of HV battery warnings or reduced electric range
* No evidence of regular charging — the hybrid battery may have been neglected
* Any sign of timing chain rattle on cold start
* Poor service history — the E-Pace PHEV is subject to unexpected component failures

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Jaguar E-Pace P300e is a compelling plug‑in hybrid SUV, combining the PT153 1.5‑litre three‑cylinder turbo petrol engine with a 108 bhp electric motor for 309 PS and 540 Nm. It offers genuine electric‑only capability for short commutes (up to 33 miles) and impressive performance (0‑60 mph in 6.1‑6.5 seconds). However, PHEV complexity brings potential issues — a safety recall for the coolant pump drive belt, software and hybrid system failures, HV battery degradation, and timing chain tensioner wear. The petrol models are better than the diesels, but the PHEVs do have some fairly common issues. Buy on service history above all else — a vehicle with documented recall completion, battery health checks, and regular maintenance is a safe bet; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare E-Pace P300e engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar E-Pace P300e (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Recall Status — Is recall R/2023/088 (coolant pump belt) completed? This is safety‑critical
2. Software Update — Is recall H555 (inverter/engine software) completed?
3. Service History — Full history with correct hybrid system maintenance
4. HV Battery Health — Has the battery been tested? What's its state of health?
5. Electric Range — Does the displayed range match expectations? (33 miles claimed, real‑world ~20‑27 miles)
6. EV Mode — Does the car enter EV mode correctly? Any hesitation or erratic behaviour?
7. Cold Start — Any rattling? (timing chain tensioner warning)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
